answer:
Definition

Architecting an API Gateway for 1M+ users means designing a horizontally scalable entry point that handles authentication, rate limiting, request routing, and response aggregation — while maintaining sub-100ms p99 latency under peak load.

Concrete Use-Case

In the HR Leave Management System, Manoj would evolve the gateway from a single Fastify reverse-proxy into a clustered gateway layer. At 1M users, each department submits leave requests simultaneously at month-end. The gateway fans out to leave-service, approval-service, and notification-service, aggregating responses. A BFF layer composes Angular dashboard payloads, reducing 6 round-trips to 1.

SVG Diagram

Code Example

// Gateway cluster with consistent hashing
import Fastify from 'fastify';
import { createClient } from 'redis';

const app = Fastify({ logger: true });
const redis = createClient({ url: process.env.REDIS_URL });

// Rate limiting per tenant (sliding window)
app.addHook('onRequest', async (req, reply) => {
  const tenantId = req.headers['x-tenant-id'] || 'default';
  const key = `ratelimit:${tenantId}:${Math.floor(Date.now() / 60000)}`;
  const count = await redis.incr(key);
  if (count === 1) await redis.expire(key, 120);
  if (count > 1000) {
    reply.code(429).send({ error: 'Rate limit exceeded' });
    return;
  }
  reply.header('X-RateLimit-Remaining', 1000 - count);
});

// Fan-out aggregation for dashboard
app.get('/api/dashboard', async (req) => {
  const userId = req.user.id;
  const [leaves, approvals, notifications] = await Promise.all([
    fetch(`${LEAVE_SVC}/leaves?userId=${userId}`).then(r => r.json()),
    fetch(`${APPROVAL_SVC}/pending?approverId=${userId}`).then(r => r.json()),
    fetch(`${NOTIFY_SVC}/unread?userId=${userId}`).then(r => r.json()),
  ]);
  return { leaves, approvals, notifications };
});

// Circuit breaker for downstream services
const circuitState = new Map();
function withCircuitBreaker(serviceName, fn) {
  const state = circuitState.get(serviceName) || { failures: 0, open: false, lastAttempt: 0 };
  if (state.open && Date.now() - state.lastAttempt < 30000) {
    throw new Error(`Circuit open for ${serviceName}`);
  }
  state.open = false;
  circuitState.set(serviceName, state);
  return fn().catch(err => {
    state.failures++;
    if (state.failures >= 5) { state.open = true; state.lastAttempt = Date.now(); }
    throw err;
  });
}

Debugging Narrative

Situation: During a company-wide leave submission window (500+ concurrent requests), the HR system gateway started returning 502 errors. Manoj noticed p99 latency spiked from 80ms to 12 seconds.

Investigation: Checked Fastify logs — the approval-service was timing out. Used netstat to find 800+ ESTABLISHED connections to the approval-service port — connection pool exhaustion. The gateway was creating a new HTTP connection per request instead of reusing.

Fix: Switched to undici connection pooling with maxConnections: 50 per upstream service. Added circuit breaker logic (5 failures → 30s open). Added Redis-based response caching for read-heavy dashboard calls (TTL 10s). Result: p99 dropped to 45ms, zero 502s in next month-end cycle.

Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
Gateway becomes single point of failureRun 3+ gateway instances behind a load balancer with health checks
Fan-out amplification (1 client request → N backend calls)Add response caching, use BFF pattern to batch queries
Connection pool exhaustion under loadUse connection pooling (undici), set maxConnections per upstream
Cascading failures from slow downstreamCircuit breaker + timeout per service (5s max)

Validation Strategy

  • Load test with k6: ramp to 10K concurrent users, verify p99 < 100ms
  • Chaos test: kill one gateway instance, verify zero-downtime failover
  • Circuit breaker test: inject 5s latency into approval-service, verify gateway returns cached/degraded response
  • Rate limit test: send 1001 requests in 1 minute from same tenant, verify 429 on request 1001

Definition

API Gateway performance bottlenecks arise when the gateway layer — responsible for routing, auth, rate limiting, and aggregation — becomes the throughput ceiling. Common bottlenecks include connection pool exhaustion, synchronous auth validation, uncompressed payloads, missing response caches, and inefficient request serialization.

Concrete Use-Case

In the CRM Sales Dashboard, Manoj's Express.js gateway handled 12 API routes serving the sales pipeline. During quarterly reporting, all 200 sales reps hit the dashboard simultaneously. The gateway serialized JWT validation synchronously, blocking the event loop for 15ms per request — creating a 3-second queue at 200 concurrent users.

SVG Diagram

Code Example

// Before: Synchronous JWT blocking event loop
app.use((req, res, next) => {
  const token = req.headers.authorization?.split(' ')[1];
  try {
    req.user = jwt.verify(token, SECRET); // CPU-bound, blocks event loop
    next();
  } catch { res.status(401).json({ error: 'Invalid token' }); }
});

// After: Async verification with JWKS caching
import { createRemoteJWKSet, jwtVerify } from 'jose';

const JWKS = createRemoteJWKSet(
  new URL(`${AUTH_ISSUER}/.well-known/jwks.json`),
  { cacheMaxAge: 600_000 } // Cache JWKS for 10 min
);

app.addHook('onRequest', async (req, reply) => {
  const token = req.headers.authorization?.split(' ')[1];
  if (!token) return reply.code(401).send({ error: 'Missing token' });
  try {
    const { payload } = await jwtVerify(token, JWKS, {
      issuer: AUTH_ISSUER,
      audience: 'hr-leave-api',
    });
    req.user = payload;
  } catch (err) {
    reply.code(401).send({ error: 'Token verification failed' });
  }
});

// Response cache middleware
app.addHook('onRequest', async (req, reply) => {
  if (req.method !== 'GET') return;
  const cacheKey = `cache:${req.url}:${req.user?.id}`;
  const cached = await redis.get(cacheKey);
  if (cached) {
    reply.header('X-Cache', 'HIT').send(JSON.parse(cached));
    return;
  }
});

app.addHook('onSend', async (req, reply, payload) => {
  if (req.method === 'GET' && reply.statusCode === 200) {
    const cacheKey = `cache:${req.url}:${req.user?.id}`;
    await redis.setEx(cacheKey, 10, payload); // 10s TTL
  }
});

Debugging Narrative

Situation: CRM Dashboard response times degraded from 200ms to 4s during quarterly report generation. Sales managers complained the pipeline view was unusable.

Investigation: Manoj added Fastify request timing hooks. Breakdown: JWT verify 15ms (×200 concurrent = event loop blocked), downstream fan-out 800ms (3 services, sequential), JSON serialization 200ms (2MB payload). Used clinic doctor — confirmed event loop delay spikes to 500ms.

Resolution: (1) Switched JWT from jsonwebtoken sync to jose async with JWKS caching, cutting verify to 2ms. (2) Parallelized fan-out with Promise.allSettled — 800ms → 300ms. (3) Added gzip compression — 2MB → 400KB. (4) Added 10s Redis response cache for dashboard reads. Net result: p95 dropped from 4s to 180ms.

Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
Sync JWT verify blocks Node.js event loopUse async jose library with JWKS caching
Sequential fan-out multiplies latencyPromise.allSettled for parallel calls + timeout per service
Large JSON payloads saturate bandwidthEnable gzip/brotli compression, paginate responses
Cache stampede on TTL expiryUse stale-while-revalidate pattern or jitter TTLs

Validation Strategy

  • Benchmark JWT verify: measure event loop delay with clinic doctor before/after async switch
  • Load test fan-out: simulate 500 concurrent dashboard requests, verify p99 < 500ms
  • Cache hit ratio: monitor Redis cache hit rate, target >80% for read-heavy endpoints
  • Payload size audit: log response sizes, alert if any response exceeds 1MB uncompressed

Definition

The Backend-for-Frontend (BFF) Pattern at scale means creating dedicated API layers for each client type (web, mobile, admin), each tailored to compose and transform data from shared microservices. For 1M+ users, each BFF must independently scale, cache, and degrade gracefully.

Concrete Use-Case

In ContactsHub, Manoj would architect separate BFFs: a Web BFF (React SPA — serves paginated contacts with Cloudinary image URLs), a Mobile BFF (returns compressed thumbnails + offline sync payloads), and an Admin BFF (bulk operations, CSV exports). Each BFF calls the same Fastify contacts-service and auth-service but shapes responses differently.

SVG Diagram

Code Example

// Web BFF — optimized for React SPA dashboard
import Fastify from 'fastify';
const app = Fastify();

app.get('/api/web/contacts', async (req) => {
  const { page = 1, search = '' } = req.query;
  const [contacts, totalCount] = await Promise.all([
    contactService.search({ search, page, limit: 20 }),
    contactService.count({ search }),
  ]);
  // Transform for React: include full Cloudinary URLs, formatted dates
  return {
    contacts: contacts.map(c => ({
      ...c,
      avatar: c.avatarId
        ? `https://res.cloudinary.com/${CLOUD}/image/upload/w_80,h_80,c_fill/${c.avatarId}`
        : null,
      createdAt: new Intl.DateTimeFormat('en-IN').format(c.createdAt),
    })),
    pagination: { page, totalPages: Math.ceil(totalCount / 20), totalCount },
  };
});

// Mobile BFF — compressed payloads, delta sync
app.get('/api/mobile/contacts/sync', async (req) => {
  const { lastSyncTimestamp } = req.query;
  const delta = await contactService.getModifiedSince(lastSyncTimestamp);
  return {
    contacts: delta.map(c => ({
      id: c.id,
      name: c.name,
      phone: c.phone,
      thumbUrl: c.avatarId
        ? `https://res.cloudinary.com/${CLOUD}/image/upload/w_40,q_60/${c.avatarId}`
        : null,
    })),
    syncTimestamp: Date.now(),
    hasMore: delta.length === 100,
  };
});

// Admin BFF — bulk operations
app.post('/api/admin/contacts/export', async (req) => {
  const { filters, format } = req.body;
  const stream = contactService.streamAll(filters);
  return format === 'csv'
    ? reply.type('text/csv').send(toCsvStream(stream))
    : reply.send(stream);
});

Debugging Narrative

Situation: ContactsHub mobile users reported sync taking 30+ seconds. Web users were fine. The mobile app was calling the same API as the web SPA.

Investigation: Manoj traced the mobile requests — each sync returned full contact objects (40 fields, including base64-encoded avatars) even though the mobile app only displayed name, phone, and thumbnail. Payload per sync: 8MB for 500 contacts.

Fix: Created a dedicated Mobile BFF endpoint that returned only 4 fields per contact and used Cloudinary's on-the-fly resizing (40px width, quality 60). Payload dropped from 8MB to 120KB. Added delta sync — only contacts modified since lastSyncTimestamp. Mobile sync time: 30s → 800ms.

Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
BFF code duplication across clientsExtract shared service clients into an internal SDK package
BFF becomes a monolith itselfKeep each BFF focused on orchestration + transformation only
Inconsistent business logic across BFFsBusiness rules live in domain services, BFFs only shape data
Scaling BFFs independently adds ops overheadContainerize each BFF, use Render auto-scaling per service

Validation Strategy

  • Payload audit: assert mobile BFF response < 200KB for 500 contacts
  • Contract test: verify each BFF returns expected schema with Pact
  • Independence test: take down Mobile BFF, verify Web BFF unaffected
  • Performance: load test each BFF independently at 5K RPS

Definition

BFF Pattern performance bottlenecks emerge when the Backend-for-Frontend layer introduces unnecessary serialization, duplicated downstream calls, or becomes a monolithic aggregation point that blocks under load. Key bottleneck areas: response composition latency, N+1 downstream calls, payload over-fetching, and cache invalidation storms.

Concrete Use-Case

In the HR Leave Management System, the Angular dashboard BFF composed data from 4 services (leaves, approvals, calendar, notifications). During month-end, 500 managers opened the dashboard simultaneously. The BFF made 4 sequential HTTP calls per request (2s total), then merged and re-serialized a 3MB JSON response — hitting a 10-second wall.

SVG Diagram

Code Example

// Before: Sequential calls in BFF
app.get('/api/bff/manager-dashboard', async (req) => {
  const leaves = await fetch(`${LEAVE_SVC}/summary`).then(r => r.json());
  const approvals = await fetch(`${APPROVAL_SVC}/pending`).then(r => r.json());
  const calendar = await fetch(`${CALENDAR_SVC}/events`).then(r => r.json());
  const notifs = await fetch(`${NOTIFY_SVC}/unread`).then(r => r.json());
  return { leaves, approvals, calendar, notifs }; // 2000ms total
});

// After: Parallel with caching + timeout
app.get('/api/bff/manager-dashboard', async (req) => {
  const userId = req.user.id;
  const cacheKey = `dashboard:${userId}`;
  const cached = await redis.get(cacheKey);
  if (cached) return JSON.parse(cached);

  const fetchWithTimeout = (url, ms = 3000) =>
    Promise.race([
      fetch(url).then(r => r.json()),
      new Promise((_, reject) => setTimeout(() => reject(new Error('timeout')), ms)),
    ]);

  const results = await Promise.allSettled([
    fetchWithTimeout(`${LEAVE_SVC}/summary?userId=${userId}`),
    fetchWithTimeout(`${APPROVAL_SVC}/pending?approverId=${userId}`),
    fetchWithTimeout(`${CALENDAR_SVC}/events?userId=${userId}`),
    fetchWithTimeout(`${NOTIFY_SVC}/unread?userId=${userId}`),
  ]);

  const response = {
    leaves: results[0].status === 'fulfilled' ? results[0].value : { error: 'unavailable' },
    approvals: results[1].status === 'fulfilled' ? results[1].value : { error: 'unavailable' },
    calendar: results[2].status === 'fulfilled' ? results[2].value : { error: 'unavailable' },
    notifs: results[3].status === 'fulfilled' ? results[3].value : { error: 'unavailable' },
  };

  await redis.setEx(cacheKey, 15, JSON.stringify(response));
  return response; // 280ms total
});

Debugging Narrative

Situation: HR managers reported the dashboard took 10+ seconds to load at month-end. Backend services individually responded in <500ms each.

Investigation: Manoj added request tracing spans to the BFF. Found: 4 sequential HTTP calls (500ms each = 2000ms), followed by JSON.stringify on a 3MB combined payload (200ms), followed by gzip compression (150ms). Under 500 concurrent users, event loop delay reached 800ms due to synchronous JSON serialization.

Fix: (1) Parallelized all 4 downstream calls with Promise.allSettled (2000ms → 500ms). (2) Added 15s Redis cache for dashboard data. (3) Used streaming JSON serialization (fast-json-stringify with schema). (4) Added Promise.race timeout of 3s per service with graceful degradation. Result: p95 from 10s → 350ms.

Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
Sequential downstream calls multiply latencyPromise.allSettled for parallel fan-out
One slow service blocks entire responseTimeout per service + graceful degradation (return partial data)
Large response serialization blocks event loopUse fast-json-stringify with pre-compiled schemas
Cache thundering herd on expiryStale-while-revalidate: serve stale data while refreshing in background

Validation Strategy

  • Distributed tracing: verify all 4 downstream calls execute in parallel (same time span)
  • Load test: 500 concurrent dashboard requests, p95 < 500ms
  • Degradation test: kill notification-service, verify dashboard still loads with partial data
  • Cache hit ratio: monitor Redis hits, expect >70% during peak hours

Definition

A Caching Strategy deep dive at the advanced level covers multi-layer caching (browser → CDN → reverse proxy → application → database), cache invalidation patterns (TTL, event-driven, write-through, write-behind), failure modes (thundering herd, stale data serving, cache poisoning), and rollback strategies when caching introduces consistency bugs.

Concrete Use-Case

In the HR Leave Management System, Manoj implemented a 3-layer cache: (1) Angular's HTTP interceptor cached leave balance lookups for 30s in memory, (2) Redis cached the full leave calendar per department for 60s, (3) PostgreSQL materialized views pre-computed monthly leave summaries. The challenge: when a manager approves a leave, all 3 layers must invalidate consistently — otherwise employees see stale balances and book overlapping leaves.

SVG Diagram

Code Example

// Event-driven cache invalidation
import { createClient } from 'redis';
const redis = createClient({ url: process.env.REDIS_URL });
const subscriber = redis.duplicate();

// On leave approval — write-through + publish
async function approveLeave(leaveId: string, approverId: string) {
  const leave = await prisma.$transaction(async (tx) => {
    const updated = await tx.leave.update({
      where: { id: leaveId },
      data: { status: 'APPROVED', approvedBy: approverId, approvedAt: new Date() },
    });
    // Refresh materialized view in same transaction
    await tx.$executeRaw`REFRESH MATERIALIZED VIEW CONCURRENTLY leave_summary`;
    return updated;
  });

  // Invalidate Redis cache
  const keys = [
    `leave-balance:${leave.employeeId}`,
    `dept-calendar:${leave.departmentId}`,
    `pending-approvals:${approverId}`,
  ];
  await redis.del(keys);

  // Notify all BFF instances via pub/sub
  await redis.publish('cache-invalidation', JSON.stringify({
    type: 'leave-approved',
    keys,
    employeeId: leave.employeeId,
  }));

  // Notify Angular client via Socket.io
  io.to(`user:${leave.employeeId}`).emit('cache-bust', {
    resource: 'leave-balance',
    timestamp: Date.now(),
  });

  return leave;
}

// Thundering herd protection — single-flight pattern
const inFlight = new Map();
async function cachedGet(key, fetcher, ttl = 60) {
  const cached = await redis.get(key);
  if (cached) return JSON.parse(cached);

  if (inFlight.has(key)) return inFlight.get(key);

  const promise = fetcher().then(async (data) => {
    await redis.setEx(key, ttl, JSON.stringify(data));
    inFlight.delete(key);
    return data;
  });

  inFlight.set(key, promise);
  return promise;
}

Debugging Narrative

Situation: After deploying Redis caching for leave balances, employees reported their balance not updating after a leave was approved. Some employees booked overlapping leaves because the stale cache showed available days.

Investigation: Manoj found the write path updated PostgreSQL and published a Socket.io event to the employee — but the Redis cache key for the department calendar was NOT being invalidated. The approval function only cleared the employee's personal balance key. Other employees in the same department still saw the old calendar.

Fix: Added department-level cache invalidation. Created a cache-invalidation Redis pub/sub channel so all gateway instances (not just the one processing the approval) clear their local caches. Added a cache version counter pattern — each department has a dept:{id}:version key that increments on any change, and all cache keys include the version.

Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
Stale cache serves outdated data after writesEvent-driven invalidation + pub/sub to all instances
Thundering herd on cache missSingle-flight pattern: coalesce concurrent requests for same key
Cache poisoning from malformed responsesValidate response schema before caching; never cache error responses
Materialized view refresh blocks writesUse CONCURRENTLY refresh; schedule during off-peak or on event

Validation Strategy

  • Consistency test: approve a leave, immediately query balance from 3 different API instances — all must reflect updated balance within 2s
  • Thundering herd test: expire a popular cache key, send 100 simultaneous requests — only 1 should hit the database
  • Rollback drill: disable Redis via feature flag, verify app falls back to direct DB queries within 1 request
  • Cache hit ratio dashboard: alert if hit ratio drops below 60%

Definition

Architecting a Caching Strategy for 1M+ users requires a multi-tier approach: edge caching (CDN) for static and semi-static content, distributed application cache (Redis Cluster) for session/API data, local in-process cache for hot configuration, and database-level caching (materialized views, query cache). The strategy must handle cache coherence across regions, graceful degradation when the cache tier fails, and capacity planning for memory budgets.

Concrete Use-Case

In a scaled CRM Sales Dashboard, 1M sales reps access pipeline data. Manoj would design: CDN caches the Angular bundle + chart assets (immutable hashes), Redis Cluster stores per-user pipeline summaries (TTL 30s), and MongoDB's aggregation pipeline results are cached as materialized collections refreshed every 5 minutes. The key challenge: when a deal status changes, the pipeline summary for that rep must update within 5 seconds.

SVG Diagram

Code Example

// Multi-tier cache with fallback chain
import { LRUCache } from 'lru-cache';
import { createCluster } from 'redis';

const localCache = new LRUCache({ max: 5000, ttl: 60_000 });
const redisCluster = createCluster({
  rootNodes: [
    { url: 'redis://node1:6379' },
    { url: 'redis://node2:6379' },
    { url: 'redis://node3:6379' },
  ],
});

async function tieredGet(key: string, fetcher: () => Promise, opts = { localTtl: 60, redisTtl: 30 }): Promise {
  // Tier 1: In-process LRU
  const local = localCache.get(key) as T | undefined;
  if (local) return local;

  // Tier 2: Redis Cluster
  try {
    const redisVal = await redisCluster.get(key);
    if (redisVal) {
      const parsed = JSON.parse(redisVal) as T;
      localCache.set(key, parsed, { ttl: opts.localTtl * 1000 });
      return parsed;
    }
  } catch (err) {
    console.warn('Redis unavailable, falling through to DB', err);
  }

  // Tier 3: Database (source of truth)
  const data = await fetcher();
  localCache.set(key, data, { ttl: opts.localTtl * 1000 });
  try {
    await redisCluster.setEx(key, opts.redisTtl, JSON.stringify(data));
  } catch { /* Redis write failure — non-fatal */ }
  return data;
}

// Usage in route handler
app.get('/api/pipeline/:userId', async (req) => {
  return tieredGet(
    `pipeline:${req.params.userId}`,
    () => db.collection('deals').aggregate([
      { $match: { ownerId: req.params.userId } },
      { $group: { _id: '$stage', total: { $sum: '$value' }, count: { $sum: 1 } } },
    ]).toArray(),
    { localTtl: 10, redisTtl: 30 },
  );
});

Debugging Narrative

Situation: At 100K concurrent users, Redis memory usage grew to 12GB (budget: 8GB) and eviction started. Cache hit ratio dropped from 85% to 40%, causing a flood of DB queries that saturated the connection pool.

Investigation: Manoj ran redis-cli --bigkeys — found that pipeline summary cache entries averaged 8KB each (expected 2KB). The aggregation results included raw deal arrays instead of just stage summaries. Additionally, TTLs weren't being set for some keys due to a race condition in the write path.

Fix: (1) Changed the aggregation to return only summary stats (8KB → 1.5KB per entry). (2) Fixed the TTL race condition by using setEx instead of separate set + expire. (3) Added maxmemory-policy allkeys-lfu to evict least-frequently-used keys. (4) Added a memory usage alert at 70% capacity. Result: 12GB → 3GB, hit ratio back to 88%.

Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
Redis memory grows unboundedSet maxmemory + allkeys-lfu policy; monitor with alerts at 70%
Cache miss storm overwhelms databaseSingle-flight pattern + circuit breaker on DB connections
Inconsistent TTLs cause unpredictable behaviorUse setEx (atomic set+expire); centralize TTL config
Local cache diverges from Redis after invalidationSubscribe to Redis pub/sub invalidation channel; clear local cache on event

Validation Strategy

  • Memory budget test: populate cache for 1M simulated users, verify total memory < 4GB
  • Failover test: kill 1 Redis cluster node, verify reads still succeed from replicas
  • Hit ratio monitoring: dashboard showing per-endpoint cache hit ratio, alert below 60%
  • Eviction test: fill cache to maxmemory, verify LFU eviction keeps hot keys available
